metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify where 'Four-Criterion test', 'Rule-5 structural-artifact exception', and 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag' are defined (file path + section), or include their definitions inline in the plan if they are new.
findings.finding: The plan references 'Four-Criterion test', 'Rule-5 structural-artifact exception', and 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag' as named, pre-existing FP-discrimination protocols to be imported into the overlay, but no file in the codebase defines or documents these protocols anywhere — they are unverifiable assertions.
findings.location: What section: 'FP-guards: Four-Criterion test, Rule-5 structural-artifact exception, VALID-patterns-not-to-flag'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the file/section where 'Base NOT-flag rules' are defined (or clarify they are to be authored as part of this plan), so the carve-out exception has a concrete referent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings' as a governing principle, but no source file (code-review-base.md, code-review-verify.md, criteria_routing.json, or any other) defines 'Base NOT-flag rules', making this assertion unverifiable and potentially introducing a silent policy gap.
findings.location: What section: 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify (by file path or ticket reference) the authoritative definitions of 'Four-Criterion test', 'Rule-5 structural-artifact exception', and 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag' so the implementer can apply them correctly rather than invent them.
findings.finding: Three named FP-guard protocols referenced in the plan ('Four-Criterion test', 'Rule-5 structural-artifact exception', 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag') are not defined or cited anywhere in the codebase; a search across all of src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ and eval_specs/ finds no matches. The mechanism requires the implementer to author FP-guard content against these protocols, but they are opaque — if the implementer doesn't have the definition source, the guard logic will be invented rather than applied correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what 'Base NOT-flag rules' refers to (file path or section) so the carve-out 'do not apply to test-correctness findings' is actionable for the implementer.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings' without identifying what the 'Base NOT-flag rules' are or where they live. The code-review-base.md overlay and code-review-verify.md have no such named rule set. An implementer following this constraint cannot apply it correctly without knowing what it excludes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Four-Criterion test, Rule-5 structural-artifact exception, and VALID-patterns-not-to-flag in the plan (or a linked spec), including the acceptance criteria each applies to and the decision rule an LLM executor can follow.
findings.finding: The three FP-guard constructs (Four-Criterion test, Rule-5 structural-artifact exception, VALID-patterns-not-to-flag) are referenced as named rubric terms but are defined nowhere in the plan or codebase; the executor cannot implement them.
findings.location: Plan § What — 'FP-guards: Four-Criterion test, Rule-5 structural-artifact exception, VALID-patterns-not-to-flag'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or link to) the 'base NOT-flag rules' the plan says do not apply to test-correctness findings, so the override boundary is explicit and implementable.
findings.finding: The phrase 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings' references a named rule set ('base NOT-flag rules') that is defined nowhere in the plan or codebase, leaving the decision boundary between base-suppressed and overlay-applicable findings unspecified.
findings.location: Plan § What — 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale (inline comment, ADR stub, or CLAUDE.md note) for the 'rubric lives in overlay, not verifier' architecture, documenting why a shared sidecar or verifier-side rubric was considered and rejected — this justifies committing the pattern for all six overlays.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'overlay carries domain rubric, verifier does not' as a locked principle and explicitly positions this ticket as establishing the pattern for five subsequent overlays, but offers no justification for this architecture over alternatives (e.g., a shared rubric sidecar imported by all overlays, inline verifier rules, or a DSL-based rubric file). A pattern that is being committed as the template for an entire family of overlays warrants at least a brief documented rationale against the next-most-obvious alternative.
findings.location: What section — 'locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier' / 'DO THIS FIRST to prove the overlay-authoring + eval pattern before the other five'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the decision logic for each of the five recall anti-patterns (change-detector, tautological, over/under-mocking, source-grepping, existence-only) in the plan — what code/test shape triggers each, and what distinguishes it from a valid test.
findings.finding: The five recall anti-pattern names (change-detector, tautological, over/under-mocking, source-grepping, existence-only) are referenced in the plan but their decision logic — what constitutes each pattern and when to fire — is never defined anywhere in the plan or codebase.
findings.location: Plan § What — 'Recall: change-detector, tautological, over/under-mocking, source-grepping, existence-only'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Pattern vocabulary' subsection to the plan (or a companion spec doc referenced by the plan) that for each pattern names: (a) the triggering code shape, (b) the harm it causes, and (c) at least one concrete example that fires and one that does not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that code-review-tests.eval.yaml is being created from scratch, name the eval-spec format/schema it must conform to, and define the scorer + threshold that constitute 'passing'.
findings.finding: AC2 requires 'a passing recall + no-fire eval' for the tests overlay, but no code-review-tests.eval.yaml (or any code-review-*.eval.yaml) exists in src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/ — the entire eval harness for code-review overlays is absent, so 'Lands with a passing eval' is an unverifiable assertion about an existing infrastructure that does not yet exist.
findings.location: AC2: 'Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval; does not over-suppress valid patterns.' / Files section: 'eval spec + fixtures for the tests overlay'
findings.suggested_fix: Point to an existing eval spec (e.g. src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-finder.eval.yaml) as the format template and specify the required fields (prompt, dataset, scorers, at_least threshold) for the new code-review-tests.eval.yaml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md section or lightweight ADR capturing the overlay-vs-verifier contract (domain rubric → overlay only; verifier stays generic) so all five subsequent overlays have a recorded, referenceable design decision.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural contract — overlay carries domain rubric, generic verifier does not — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## What / AC3 ('Overlay remains advisory; no domain rubric leaks into code-review-verify.md')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CLAUDE.md entry (or `docs/adr/overlay-verifier-separation.md`) stating: 'Domain-specific recall patterns and FP-guards live exclusively in per-domain overlay prompts (e.g. code-review-tests.md). The generic Pass-2 verifier (code-review-verify.md) must remain domain-agnostic. Overlays are advisory; they do not modify the verifier.' Reference this from the plan's Files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite the file/section where the 'domain rubric lives in the overlay' principle is documented, or add a note that this plan is the first to codify it and specify where it will be recorded (e.g. an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry).
findings.finding: The plan states 'the locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier' as an established, binding architectural rule, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md, README, or design document in the repo records this principle — it is an unverifiable assertion about the codebase's governing contracts.
findings.location: What section: 'per the locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive design rationale explaining WHY domain rubric belongs in the overlay and not in code-review-verify.md (e.g. the verifier is dimension-agnostic by design; overlays are the correct extension point).
findings.finding: The plan states a sequencing rationale ('prove the overlay-authoring + eval pattern before the other five') but does not state a positive design rationale for WHY FP-guards belong in the overlay rather than anywhere else (e.g. shared rubric, or verifier). The 'locked principle' is asserted by fiat. Without a stated rationale, future authors of the other five overlays cannot apply the principle confidently.
findings.location: ## What (plan body) — 'per the locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'Rationale: the Pass-2 verifier (code-review-verify.md) is intentionally dimension-agnostic — it re-grounds any finding regardless of its dimension. Domain-specific detection logic (what to flag, what not to flag) belongs in the overlay so each dimension's rubric is co-located, independently evolvable, and does not inflate the verifier's scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign a concrete file path to the new eval spec + fixtures artifact (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml`) and acknowledge it as a CREATION (new file), not an edit of an existing file.
findings.finding: The plan's second named file — 'eval spec + fixtures for the tests overlay (recall + no-fire cases)' — does not exist anywhere in the repo; no `code-review-tests.eval.yaml` is present under src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/ or elsewhere, making this a CREATION target, not an edit target, and the plan gives it no concrete file path.
findings.location: Files section: 'eval spec + fixtures for the tests overlay (recall + no-fire cases)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml` as the explicit path in the Files list and annotate it as '(new)' to signal creation intent, consistent with how peer eval specs are named.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in AC2 the minimum fixture count (e.g. ≥1 fixture per recall pattern, ≥1 no-fire fixture per FP-guard), the pass threshold (e.g. 100% recall, 0% false-positive), and what 'does not over-suppress' means concretely (e.g. named VALID-patterns list with at least N entries).
findings.finding: AC2 defers the definition of 'passing' to the executor: the eval spec and its fixtures are created as part of this very work, so the pass threshold, fixture count, and pass rate are all unchosen design decisions the executor must make rather than implement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC2: 'Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval; does not over-suppress valid patterns.'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC2 as: 'Eval spec contains ≥1 fixture per named recall pattern (5 total) and ≥1 no-fire fixture per FP-guard (3 total); eval run achieves 100% recall fixture hit-rate and 0% no-fire false-positive rate when executed via [named command].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC: e.g. for AC2 specify the exact CLI or script (e.g. `rebar eval tests-overlay` or `python -m pytest evals/tests_overlay/`) that exercises the eval fixtures and produces a pass/fail exit code.
findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any acceptance criterion; the plan names no tool, script, or CLI invocation that would produce evidence of success for AC1, AC2, or AC3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – all three ACs; Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Unify terminology: pick one name for the FP prevention content ('FP-guards' or 'FP-discrimination guards') and one name for the valid-but-suppressed patterns ('no-fire cases' or 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag') and use it consistently across the What section and all ACs.
findings.finding: Terminology drift: the What section calls the false-positive prevention content 'FP-guards' while AC1 calls the same concept 'FP-discrimination guards'; similarly, the What section uses 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag' for the no-fire concept while AC2 calls these 'no-fire cases'. An implementer verifying AC1 and AC2 must guess whether these are the same entities.
findings.location: What section vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1, AC2)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'FP-guards' with 'FP-discrimination guards' (matching AC1) and replace 'VALID-patterns-not-to-flag' with 'no-fire cases' (matching AC2); or update the ACs to match the What — either direction works as long as it is consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings' is new behavior or a change to existing behavior, and if the latter, acknowledge which callers/pipeline stages currently depend on those rules applying to test findings and how they are updated.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings' as a principle, which appears to carve out an exception to existing review pipeline behavior. If code-review-verify.md or the broader review system currently applies base NOT-flag rules universally (including to test-correctness findings), this is a behavior change. The plan does not acknowledge who currently depends on the old behavior, nor does it describe how the change is communicated to or handled by those callers.
findings.location: ## What — 'Base NOT-flag rules do not apply to test-correctness findings'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note in the plan: either 'This is new behavior (overlay previously had no test-correctness domain logic)' or 'This changes existing behavior; code-review-verify.md and any pipeline stage that currently suppresses test-correctness flags via base NOT-flag rules must be audited.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the eval spec schema to the plan: specify the scorer names (e.g. recall_on_seeded_defects, no_fire_on_good_cases), minimum fixture counts, epoch/gate thresholds, and at least one concrete recall fixture and one no-fire fixture for the tests overlay.
findings.finding: The eval spec file for the tests overlay (expected at src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml or rebar-code-review-tests.eval.yaml) does not exist, and the plan does not specify the scorer names, fixture structure, or pass/fail thresholds needed to author it — leaving AC 2 ('passing recall + no-fire eval') with no runnable specification.
findings.location: Plan § Files — 'eval spec + fixtures for the tests overlay (recall + no-fire cases)' and AC 2
findings.suggested_fix: Model the spec on src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-finder.eval.yaml: define `prompt: rebar-code-review-tests`, `scorers: [recall_on_seeded_defects, no_fire_on_good_cases]`, and seed dataset entries for each of the five recall anti-patterns (one fire case each) and at least two no-fire cases (e.g. structural grep as a valid cross-language test, an existence test that is sufficient for a pure-scaffolding change).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
